   \(\cdots \to K(\mathcal{U})_1 \to K(\mathcal{U})_0 \to \mathcal{O}_U \to 0\) is exact as a complex

8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.

16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. 158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

63	
64	# Blueprint Writer
65	
66	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
67	
68	## Your Job
69	
70	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
71	
72	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Blueprint Writer Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Target chapter
83	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
84	
85	## Strategy context
86	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
87	
88	## Required content
89	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
90	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
91	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
92	- ...
93	
94	## Out of scope
95	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
96	
97	## References
98	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
99	- ...
100	
101	## Expected outcome
102	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
103	```
104	
105	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
106	
107	## Chapter format
108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{thm:another_result}
130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
132	\end{proof}
133	```
134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
167	
168	## Rules
169	
170	### What you CAN do
171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. Verify with `leandag` (see "Verifying dependencies with leandag" below) — do not eyeball it.
174	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
175	- **Write entries for Lean helper declarations.** The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: when the directive lists a helper that exists only in Lean (a prover-created lemma, an instance, a decomposition step), give it a real entry — concise informal statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}` with the exact Lean name, accurate `\uses{}`, and at least a one-line informal proof. "It's trivial" is not a reason to skip it: the entry is what carries the dependency edges and what helps a later prover fill the sorry. When the helper's Lean proof relies on a fact with no blueprint entry, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" (or create it too, if it belongs in your chapter).
176	- **Author a *Mathlib dependency anchor* and mark it `\mathlibok`** when your chapter relies on a result Mathlib already provides — write out the statement and point `\lean{}` at the real Mathlib declaration (see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below). This is the only marker you may add.
177	- **Remove a declaration block ONLY when the directive explicitly lists it for removal** (typically an isolated/orphaned statement the reviewer flagged `remove`). Default to wiring an isolated node into the graph (add the missing `\uses{}`), not deleting it — an isolated node is usually a missing edge, not dead weight. Never remove the project goal, and never remove a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
178	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
179	
180	### What you MUST do
181	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
182	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
183	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
184	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
185	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
186	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
187	
188	### What you MUST NOT do
189	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
190	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
191	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. (`\mathlibok` is different: you MAY add it, but ONLY on a Mathlib dependency anchor — see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below — never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.)
192	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
193	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
194	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
195	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
196	- **Do NOT write literal `REF` placeholders.** Never produce prose like "Definition~REF" or "Sections REF–REF". Cross-reference declarations with `\cref{<label>}` using the real label (look it up in the chapter or via `leandag`); when the target genuinely has no label yet, describe it by name in prose. The blueprint-doctor flags every literal `REF` as a malformed reference.
197	- **Do NOT interleave math delimiters.** Pick `\( … \)` for a formula and close it with `\)` — never produce `$ … \( … \) … $` or `X\(, hence \)Y` inversions; the doctor's `math-delim` lint flags every such site and the rendered output shreds mid-formula.
198	- **Do NOT paste bare label ids into prose.** "Thm.~th:main" is not a reference — write `\cref{...}` for project declarations, or the source's human-readable number ("Thm.~4.8") when quoting external material.
199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.

3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
5	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
6	% (Lemmas \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
7	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
8	% \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
9	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}) (read from

11	
12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
15	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
16	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
17	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
18	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
19	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
20	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
21	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
22	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
23	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
24	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
25	
26	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
27	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
28	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
29	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
30	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
31	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
32	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
33	\[
34	  \begin{array}{ccc}
35	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
36	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
37	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
38	  \end{array}
39	\]
40	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
41	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
42	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
43	
44	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
45

47

53	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of

68	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
69	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write

71	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
72	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
73	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
74	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
75	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
76	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
77	  \[
78	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
79	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
80	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
81	  \]

83	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
84	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
85	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
86	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
87	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
88	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
89	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
90	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
91	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
92	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
93	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
94	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
95	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).

162	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
163	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
164	
165	It is convenient to factor the construction of
166	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
167	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
168	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
169	
170	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
171	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
172	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
173	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
174	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
175	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
176	\[
177	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
178	    \;=\;
179	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
180	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
181	\]
182	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
183	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
184	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
185	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
186	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
187	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
188	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
189	
190	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
191	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
192	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
193	\[
194	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
195	  \qquad
196	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
197	\]
198	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
199	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
200	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
201	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
202	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
203	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
204	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
205	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
206	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
207	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
208	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
209	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
210	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
211	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
212	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
213	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
214	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
215	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
216	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
217	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
218	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
219	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
220	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
221	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
222	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
223	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
224	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
225	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
226	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
227	
228	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
229	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
230	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
231	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
232	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
233	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
234	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
235	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
236	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
237	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
238	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
239	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
240	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
241	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
242	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
243	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
244	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
245	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
246	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
247	
248	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
249	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
250	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
251	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
252	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
253

259	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
260	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
261	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
262	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
263	  apply.
264	\end{definition}
265

296	\begin{definition}\leanok
297	[Push--pull comparison map]
298	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
299	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap}
300	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
301	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
302	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
303	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
304	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
305	  \[
306	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
307	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
308	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
309	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
310	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
311	      \;=\;
312	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
313	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
314	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
315	  \]
316	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
317	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
318	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
319	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
320	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
321	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
322	\end{definition}
323	
324	\begin{lemma}
325	[Push--pull functor — identity law]
326	  \label{lem:push_pull_id}
327	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
328	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
329	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} respects identities:
330	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
331	\end{lemma}
332	
333	\begin{proof}
334	  A direct calculation with comparison isomorphisms, with no sectionwise content.
335	  Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward comparison as the
336	  conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison; the identity leg then collapses
337	  by the identity normalisation of the pseudofunctor together with right unitality,
338	  and the two over-triangle transport coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
339	\end{proof}

341	\begin{lemma}
342	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
343	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
344	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp}
345	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
346	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
347	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
348	  \[
349	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
350	  \]
351	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
352	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
353	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
354	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
355	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
356	\end{lemma}
357	
358	\begin{proof}
359	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
360	  The composition identity \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\) follows from three
361	  coherence facts. First, the composite adjunction unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) decomposes into
362	  the iterated units \(\eta^{g}\), \(\eta^{h}\) via the mate identity of
363	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} and the naturality of the adjunction unit. Second,
364	  the resulting pullback-side identity is the \emph{pentagon coherence} of the pullback
365	  pseudofunctor — the associativity \(2\)-cell relating the two bracketings of the comparison
366	  isomorphism \((p \circ q \circ r)^* \cong r^* q^* p^*\). Third, the over-triangle transport
367	  coherences are absorbed by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}.
368	\end{proof}

370	\begin{lemma}
371	[Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push--pull head]
372	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
373	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}
374	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
375	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a
376	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module \(N\), the adjunction unit \(\eta^p_N\) of
377	  \(p^* \dashv p_*\) at \(N\), followed by \(p_*\) of the unit \(\eta^f\) for
378	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) at \(p^*N\), followed by the pushforward comparison
379	  \(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\), equals the unit \(\eta^{f \circ p}_N\) for the
380	  composite followed by \((f \circ p)_*\) applied to the inverse pullback
381	  comparison \((f \circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\):
382	  \[
383	    \eta^p_N \;\circ\; p_*(\eta^f_{p^*N}) \;\circ\;
384	      \bigl(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\bigr)
385	    \;=\;
386	    \eta^{f \circ p}_N \;\circ\; (f \circ p)_*\bigl((f\circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\bigr)^{-1}.
387	  \]
388	  This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
389	  \(\eta^{f \circ p}\) into the iterated units \(\eta^p, \eta^f\); it is the
390	  reusable ingredient consumed by the functoriality (pentagon) law of the
391	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}.
392	\end{lemma}
393	
394	\begin{proof}
395	  A direct mate-calculus computation: unfold both sides using the definition of
396	  the adjunction unit for the composite and the pseudofunctor structure maps, then
397	  verify the equality by the mate identity for the pushforward/pullback comparison.
398	\end{proof}

400	\begin{lemma}
401	[Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push--pull tail]
402	  \label{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
403	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_transport_cancel}
404	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
405	  Let \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\), \(p_1 : Y_1 \to X\), \(p_2 : Y_2 \to X\) satisfy the
406	  over-triangle \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\), and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
407	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the pullback-comparison leg of the push--pull map,
408	  conjugated by the two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions along the over-triangle,
409	  collapses to the transport-light form
410	  \[
411	    e_1 \;\circ\; p_{2*}\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
412	      \;\circ\; e_2
413	    \;=\;
414	    (\bar g p_1)_*\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
415	      \;\circ\; e_3,
416	  \]
417	  where \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) are the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions induced by
418	  \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\). It states the over-triangle cancellation in the
419	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} with the
420	  over-triangle equality as a free hypothesis, so a single substitution turns the
421	  transports into identities; it is the reusable pre-coherence brick for the
422	  composition (pentagon) law.
423	\end{lemma}
424	
425	\begin{proof}
426	  Substitute the over-triangle equality \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\) to replace
427	  transport coercions by identities, then verify the cancellation by direct
428	  calculation with the pushforward comparison.
429	\end{proof}

\subsection{Presheaf-level {\v C}ech machinery}
\label{sec:presheaf_cech_machinery}

The {\v C}ech-acyclicity of injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} below) is not a statement about a single
affine cover: it compares the explicit {\v C}ech complex to the
injective-resolution-defined right-derived functors on the abelian category
\(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
comparison rests on three presheaf-level facts, recorded here: a free complex
\(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of presheaves that \emph{represents} the {\v C}ech
functor (Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} and
Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}); the resolution property of that
complex (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}); and the resulting
identification of {\v C}ech cohomology with a right-derived
\(\delta\)-functor (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_delta_functor_presheaves}), which needs
\(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) to have enough injectives
(Lemma~\ref{lem:presheaf_modules_enough_injectives}).

\begin{definition}
[Free presheaf complex of a cover]
  \label{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreePresheafComplex}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into}
  % (the complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$) (read from
  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1142--1162, with the $j_{p!}$ description
  % L1095--1135).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider the chain complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$
  % of presheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
  % $$
  % \ldots
  % \to
  % \bigoplus_{i_0i_1i_2} (j_{i_0i_1i_2})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0i_1i_2}}
  % \to
  % \bigoplus_{i_0i_1} (j_{i_0i_1})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0i_1}}
  % \to
  % \bigoplus_{i_0} (j_{i_0})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0}}
  % \to 0 \to \ldots
  % $$
  % where the last nonzero term is placed in degree $0$
  % and where the map
  % $$
  % (j_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}}}
  % \longrightarrow
  % (j_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}})_{p!}
  % \mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}}}
  % $$
  % is given by $(-1)^j$ times the canonical map."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
  \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into} (the free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\)).}
  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
  open covering, with \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\)
  the open immersion of each finite intersection. Write \((j)_{!}\) for the
  \emph{extension by zero} (the ``shriek'') functor on presheaves of modules along
  an open immersion \(j\): for a presheaf \(\mathcal{G}\) of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X|_U\)-modules, \((j_{!}\mathcal{G})(W) = \mathcal{G}(W)\) if
  \(W \subset U\) and \(0\) otherwise; it is the left adjoint of restriction. The
  \emph{free presheaf complex} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the chain complex
  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose term
  in degree \(p\) is the direct sum
  \[
    K(\mathcal{U})_p
      = \bigoplus_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}},
  \]
  with \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) placed in degree \(p \geq 0\) (and \(0\) in negative
  degrees), and whose differential \(K(\mathcal{U})_{p+1} \to K(\mathcal{U})_p\) is
  the alternating sum \(\sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\) of the canonical maps
  \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}} \to
  (j_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots
  \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) that drop the index \(i_j\). Each summand is a
  \emph{projective} object of \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\), because
  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0
  \ldots i_p}}, -) = (-)(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\) is the exact ``sections over
  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor; this is the structural feature exploited below.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}
[The free complex represents the {\v C}ech complex]
  \label{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechComplex_hom_identification}
  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:cech_complex}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into}
  % (statement + proof) (read from references/stacks-cohomology.tex,
  % L1163--1196).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Then there is an isomorphism
  % $$
  % \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet, \mathcal{F})
  % =
  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
  % $$
  % functorial in $\mathcal{F} \in \Ob(\textit{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X))$."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
  \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into}.}
  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
  open covering. For every presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{F}\)
  there is an isomorphism of cochain complexes of \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-modules
  \[
    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}\bigl(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
      \;=\;
    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}),
  \]
  natural in \(\mathcal{F} \in \mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\). Here the left side is
  the cochain complex obtained by applying the contravariant
  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-, \mathcal{F})\) to the chain complex
  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex},
  and the right side is the {\v C}ech complex of
  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}.
\end{lemma}

% \prod\nolimits_{i_0\ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0\ldots i_p}).
% $$
% Hence by Categories, Yoneda Lemma \ref{categories-lemma-yoneda}
% we see that there is a complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$ with terms
% as given. It is a simple matter to see that the maps are as given
% in the lemma."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:cech_complex}
  The adjunction between the shriek functor \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\) and
  restriction gives, for each multi-index, the natural identification
  \[
    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}\bigl(
      (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}},\, \mathcal{F}
    \bigr)
    = \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}}\bigl(
      \mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}},\, \mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}
    \bigr)
    = \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}).
  \]
  Taking the product over multi-indices, \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_p,
  \mathcal{F}) = \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}) =
  \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\); the direct sum
  \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) therefore represents the functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto
  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\). Under this
  identification the \((-1)^j\) index-dropping maps defining the differential of
  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) become exactly the alternating-sum restriction
  differential of \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so the
  two cochain complexes coincide; naturality in \(\mathcal{F}\) is immediate from
  the naturality of the adjunction.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[The free complex resolves \(\mathcal{O}_U\)]
  \label{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeComplex_quasiIso}
  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
  % (statement + proof) (read from references/stacks-cohomology.tex,
  % L1198--1284).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U} \subset \mathcal{O}_X$
  % be the image presheaf of the map
  % $\bigoplus j_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_i} \to \mathcal{O}_X$.
  % The chain complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$ of presheaves
  % of Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-map-into} above has homology presheaves
  % $$
  % H_i(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet) =
  % \left\{
  % \begin{matrix}
  % 0 & \text{if} & i \not = 0 \\
  % \mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U} & \text{if} & i = 0
  % \end{matrix}
  % \right.
  % $$"
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
  \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}.}
  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
  open covering. Let \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}} \subset \mathcal{O}_X\) be the
  image presheaf of the augmentation \(\bigoplus_i (j_i)_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_i}
  \to \mathcal{O}_X\) (so \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when
  \(W\) is contained in some \(U_i\), and \(0\) otherwise). Then the homology
  presheaves of the free presheaf complex
  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
  vanish away from degree \(0\) and equal \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\) in degree
  \(0\):
  \[
    H_i\bigl(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\bigr) =
    \begin{cases}
      \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}} & i = 0, \\
      0 & i \neq 0.
    \end{cases}
  \]
  Equivalently, the augmented complex \(\cdots \to K(\mathcal{U})_1 \to
  K(\mathcal{U})_0 \to \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}} \to 0\) is exact as a complex of
  presheaves; that is, \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) is a (projective) resolution of
  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}[0]\), quasi-isomorphic to \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\)
  concentrated in degree \(0\).
\end{lemma}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Consider the extended complex $K^{ext}_\bullet$ one gets by
% putting
% $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}$ in degree $-1$ with the obvious map
% $K(\mathcal{U})_0 =
% \bigoplus_{i_0} (j_{i_0})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0}} \to
% \mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}$.
% It suffices to show that taking sections of this extended complex over
% any open $W \subset X$ leads to an acyclic complex.
% In fact, we claim that for every $W \subset X$ the complex
% $K^{ext}_\bullet(W)$ is homotopy equivalent to the zero complex.
% Write $I = I_1 \amalg I_2$ where $W \subset U_i$ if and only
% if $i \in I_1$.
% ...
% If $I_1 \not = \emptyset$, then
% $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)$
% ...
% Fix an element $i_{\text{fix}} \in I_1$. Define a map
% $h : K^{ext}_p(W) \longrightarrow K^{ext}_{p + 1}(W)$
% by the rule
% $h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p + 1}} = (i_0 = i_{\text{fix}}) s_{i_1 \ldots i_p}$
% ... which is equal to $s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ as desired."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
  Exactness of a complex of presheaves is checked sectionwise, so it suffices to
  show that for every open \(W \subset X\) the extended complex of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\)-modules
  \[
    \cdots \to K(\mathcal{U})_1(W) \to K(\mathcal{U})_0(W) \to
      \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(W) \to 0
  \]
  is acyclic; in fact it is contractible. Split the index set as \(I = I_1 \amalg
  I_2\), where \(i \in I_1\) iff \(W \subset U_i\). If \(I_1 = \varnothing\) the
  extended complex is identically \(0\). If \(I_1 \neq \varnothing\) then
  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)\) and, by the explicit
  description of the shriek presheaves, \(K(\mathcal{U})_p(W) =
  \bigoplus_{i_0 \ldots i_p \in I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(W)\) with the alternating-sum
  differential. Fixing an index \(i_{\mathrm{fix}} \in I_1\), the prescription
  \[
    h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} =
      \begin{cases}
        s_{i_1 \ldots i_{p+1}} & i_0 = i_{\mathrm{fix}}, \\
        0 & i_0 \neq i_{\mathrm{fix}}
      \end{cases}
  \]
  defines a contracting homotopy: a direct computation gives \((dh + hd)(s) = s\),
  so the identity of \(K^{\mathrm{ext}}_\bullet(W)\) is homotopic to zero. Hence the
  extended complex is exact over every \(W\), which is the claimed vanishing of the
  homology presheaves.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Grothendieck abelian categories have enough injectives]
  \label{lem:grothendieck_enough_injectives}
  \lean{CategoryTheory.IsGrothendieckAbelian.enoughInjectives}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.GrothendieckCategory.EnoughInjectives}).}
  Every Grothendieck abelian category has enough injectives: each object admits a
  monomorphism into an injective object.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Module categories are Grothendieck abelian]
  \label{lem:module_cat_grothendieck}
  \lean{instIsGrothendieckAbelianModuleCat}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.AB}).}
  For a commutative (or general) ring \(R\), the category \(\mathrm{Mod}_R\) of
  \(R\)-modules is a Grothendieck abelian category.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}
[Presheaves of modules have enough injectives]
  \label{lem:presheaf_modules_enough_injectives}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.presheafModules_enoughInjectives}
  \uses{lem:grothendieck_enough_injectives, lem:module_cat_grothendieck}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma
  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-derived-presheaves} (proof, opening sentence)
  % (read from references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1317--1319).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Note that the category of presheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
  % has enough injectives, see
  % Injectives, Proposition \ref{injectives-proposition-presheaves-modules}."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-derived-presheaves} (proof).}
  The abelian category \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of presheaves of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has enough injectives: every presheaf of modules admits
  a monomorphism into an injective presheaf of modules.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:grothendieck_enough_injectives, lem:module_cat_grothendieck}
  The result is obtained through the Grothendieck-category engine. A Grothendieck
  abelian category has enough injectives
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:grothendieck_enough_injectives}), so it suffices to exhibit
  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) as a Grothendieck abelian category. A presheaf of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is a functor from the (opposite of the) poset of opens
  to module categories \(\mathrm{Mod}_{\mathcal{O}_X(W)}\), and each
  \(\mathrm{Mod}_R\) is Grothendieck abelian
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:module_cat_grothendieck}); a presheaf (functor) category valued
  in Grothendieck abelian categories is again Grothendieck abelian, since a
  generating family, exact filtered colimits and the AB5 axiom are all computed
  objectwise. The sole project-side obligation is therefore the
  \(\mathrm{IsGrothendieckAbelian}\) instance for the presheaf-of-modules category;
  once it is in place, enough injectives follows formally. (This instance is not yet
  available off the shelf and is the core to be constructed; the two Mathlib results
  cited above supply the surrounding engine.)
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[{\v C}ech cohomology is a right-derived \(\delta\)-functor on presheaves]
  \label{lem:cech_delta_functor_presheaves}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cech_delta_functor_presheaves}
  \uses{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification, lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso, lem:presheaf_modules_enough_injectives}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemmas
  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-delta-functor-presheaves} and
  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-derived-presheaves} (statement + proof)
  % (read from references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1065--1074 and L1286--1356).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The functors $\mathcal{F} \mapsto \check{H}^n(\mathcal{U},
  % \mathcal{F})$
  % form a $\delta$-functor from the abelian category of
  % presheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules to the category
  % of $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-modules (see
  % Homology, Definition
  % \ref{homology-definition-cohomological-delta-functor})."
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The {\v C}ech cohomology functors $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, -)$
  % are canonically isomorphic as a $\delta$-functor to
  % the right derived functors of the functor
  % $$
  % \check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, -) :
  % \textit{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)
  % \longrightarrow
  % \text{Mod}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}.
  % $$"
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-delta-functor-presheaves} and
  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-derived-presheaves}.}
  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
  open covering. The {\v C}ech cohomology functors \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, -)\)
  on \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) assemble into a cohomological
  \(\delta\)-functor valued in \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-modules, and this
  \(\delta\)-functor is canonically isomorphic to the right-derived functors
  \(R^p\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, -)\) of the left-exact functor
  \(\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, -) : \mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
  \mathrm{Mod}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\):
  \[
    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, -) \;\cong\; R^p\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, -)
    \qquad (p \geq 0).
  \]
  In particular, a presheaf \(\mathcal{I}\) for which
  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-, \mathcal{I})\) is exact — e.g.\ an
  injective object of \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) — has \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U},
  \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\). This is precisely what makes ``vanishing on
  injectives'' equivalent to right-derived-functor acyclicity.
\end{lemma}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Note that the category of presheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
% has enough injectives, see
% Injectives, Proposition \ref{injectives-proposition-presheaves-modules}.
% Note that $\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, -)$ is a left exact functor
% from the category of presheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
% to the category of $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-modules.
% Hence the derived functor and the right derived functor exist ...
% Let $\mathcal{I}$ be an injective presheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
% In this case the functor $\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-, \mathcal{I})$
% is exact on $\textit{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$. By
% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-map-into} we have
% $$
% \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet, \mathcal{I})
% =
% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}).
% $$
% By Lemma \ref{lemma-homology-complex} we have that $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$ is
% quasi-isomorphic to $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}[0]$. Hence by
% the exactness of Hom into $\mathcal{I}$ mentioned above we see
% that $\check{H}^i(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0$ for all
% $i > 0$. Thus the $\delta$-functor $(\check{H}^n, \delta)$
% ... is a universal $\delta$-functor.
% ... we conclude that
% $R^i\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, -) = \check{H}^i(\mathcal{U}, -)$."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification, lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso, lem:presheaf_modules_enough_injectives}
  Since taking sections \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto \mathcal{F}(W)\) is exact on
  presheaves, each {\v C}ech term \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, -)\) is an
  exact functor, so a short exact sequence of presheaves yields a short exact
  sequence of {\v C}ech complexes and hence — via the connecting maps of the long
  exact cohomology sequence — the structure of a cohomological \(\delta\)-functor on
  \(\{\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, -)\}_{p \geq 0}\); the functor
  \(\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, -)\) is left exact.

  By Lemma~\ref{lem:presheaf_modules_enough_injectives} the category
  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) has enough injectives, so the right-derived
  functors \(R^p\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, -)\) exist and form a universal
  \(\delta\)-functor. To identify the two \(\delta\)-functors it suffices, by the
  uniqueness of universal \(\delta\)-functors, to show \(\{\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U},
  -)\}\) is universal, i.e.\ effaceable in positive degrees. Let \(\mathcal{I}\) be
  an injective presheaf; then \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-, \mathcal{I})\)
  is exact. By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification},
  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet, \mathcal{I}) =
  \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I})\), and by
  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} the complex
  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) is a resolution of \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}[0]\).
  Applying the exact functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-, \mathcal{I})\)
  to this quasi-isomorphism shows the {\v C}ech complex of \(\mathcal{I}\) is exact
  in positive degrees, so \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for all
  \(p > 0\). Thus \(\{\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, -)\}\) is an effaceable, hence
  universal, \(\delta\)-functor, and by uniqueness \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, -)
  \cong R^p\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, -)\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Injective modules are {\v C}ech-acyclic]
  \label{lem:injective_cech_acyclic}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:cech_complex}
  An injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{I}\) is also injective when
  viewed as a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules: sheafification is an exact left
  adjoint to the inclusion \(\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \hookrightarrow
  \mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\), and a right adjoint whose left adjoint is exact
  preserves injectives. Now the {\v C}ech functors \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, -)\)
  are the right derived functors of the left-exact \(\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, -)\)
  on \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\), which has enough injectives. For the cover
  complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) — quasi-isomorphic to \(\mathcal{O}_U[0]\) —
  one has the identification \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}
  (K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet, \mathcal{I}) = \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
  \mathcal{I})\); since \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) is exact on
  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) for injective \(\mathcal{I}\), the {\v C}ech
  complex of \(\mathcal{I}\) is exact in positive degrees, i.e.\
  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\); in degree zero
  \(\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = \mathcal{I}(U)\) by the sheaf
  property. The presheaf-level {\v C}ech machinery this rests on — that
  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) has enough injectives and that the {\v C}ech
  complex is exact as a functor on presheaves — is developed as part of the
  chapter's foundational content.
\end{proof}
